food_subgroup: Poultry dishes — spiced and fried chicken

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Dragon Chicken or Turkish spiced fried chicken. FOODON_00002670 (chicken food product) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Chicken meat, cooked) used as closest parent; no specific code for Turkish-style spiced fried chicken exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for Dragon Chicken as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Dragon Chicken is approximately 300 g, comprising several pieces of spiced fried chicken served with accompaniments such as flatbread, salad, or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, fried, batter (USDA SR Legacy NDB 05062) adapted for Turkish spiced fried chicken preparation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Dragon Chicken is a Turkish restaurant dish of chicken pieces marinated in yoghurt and spices then deep-fried. No exact entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA for this dish. USDA SR Legacy entry for fried chicken (meat and skin, batter-coated) was used as the closest defensible analogue and adjusted modestly upward for sodium and spice-derived micronutrient contribution. Values represent a reasonable per-100g estimate for this preparation style.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; analogous estimate based on fried chicken with coating (GI ~45)
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Dragon Chicken. GI estimated at 45 (low category) based on the closest analogous food: fried chicken with a light batter/coating as reported in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The dish contains moderate carbohydrate from the marinade coating and spices; protein and fat content slow gastric emptying, supporting a low GI estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 21.6) / 100 ≈ 9.7, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 7.2 × 3 = 21.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 520 mg per 100 g; a 300 g serving contributes approximately 1560 mg sodium, representing over 60% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150 g or fewer and avoid additional salt-based condiments, as the cumulative sodium from a full restaurant serving can substantially exceed daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~45) is favourable, but the dish is energy-dense at 260 kcal per 100 g and a full restaurant serving (~300 g) delivers approximately 780 kcal, which may challenge glycaemic and weight management goals.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume a moderate portion (150–200 g) of Dragon Chicken as part of a balanced meal, pairing it with non-starchy vegetables to moderate the overall glycaemic and caloric load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 4.1 g per 100 g and cholesterol at 88 mg per 100 g, combined with deep-frying preparation, present a moderate cardiovascular risk when consumed in large portions.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Dragon Chicken to occasional consumption in portions of 150 g or less, preferring preparations where excess frying oil is drained, and balancing the meal with fibre-rich vegetables and whole grains.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dragon Chicken is a Turkish-style spiced fried chicken dish served at Turkish and Lebanese restaurants in Singapore, notably at Sultan Palace in Boat Quay. The dish consists of chicken pieces — typically bone-in thighs and drumsticks or boneless breast strips — marinated in a blend of yoghurt, red chilli, garlic, ginger, cumin, paprika, and coriander, then deep-fried until golden and crispy. The result is a dish with a boldly spiced, slightly smoky exterior and juicy, tender interior. The name 'Dragon Chicken' evokes the fiery heat of the chilli-forward marinade. Nutritionally, Dragon Chicken is a high-protein dish (approximately 22.5 g protein per 100 g) with moderate fat content (15.8 g per 100 g) derived primarily from the frying process and chicken skin. Sodium is elevated at approximately 520 mg per 100 g, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low), making it relatively favourable for blood glucose management when consumed in moderate portions. The dish is typically halal-certified in Singapore restaurant contexts and is gluten-free in its traditional preparation. It is commonly served as a main course alongside flatbread, rice, or salad, and is priced at approximately SGD 25 per serving at Singapore restaurants.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Dragon Chicken?
Is Dragon Chicken good for diabetics?
What spices are used in Dragon Chicken?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Dragon Chicken exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are adapted from the closest USDA SR Legacy analogue (fried chicken, meat and skin, batter-coated) with adjustments for the Turkish spice marinade. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed international GI tables. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Dragon Chicken is a Turkish restaurant dish sold at Sultan Palace, Boat Quay, Singapore at SGD 25 per serving. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy fried chicken data (NDB 05062 / FDC 171477) adapted for the yoghurt-and-spice marinade preparation typical of this dish. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry used for this adaptation. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water values are carried from the USDA SR Legacy analogue and are -1 only where the source does not report the nutrient; all other micronutrient values reported are from the USDA SR Legacy source. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on fried chicken with coating per Atkinson et al. 2021 (AJCN). The cuisine is classified as Turkish per the input source and restaurant context. Multilingual names provided for Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to satisfy Singaporean dish multilingual requirements, as this dish is served in Singapore. The food_id GD000310 is preserved from the input seed record.
